headline: CORN FLAT AT $4.79 AS CHINA DEAL PROPS RANGE
subheadline: Planting progress on schedule, funds wait for weather premium catalyst.
lead: Corn closed at $4.79 for a second session after Monday's China bounce, the funds digesting whether $17 billion in commitments changes the planting weather equation. Soybeans gained a penny to $12.17 on steady crush margins. The USDA says 76% of corn is planted, six points ahead of average, which takes weather premium off the table until June heat arrives. Range-bound isn't drama, but it's information: the money is positioned, just waiting for a catalyst the calendar shows.
the_takeaway: China deal holds the floor, but weather needs to cooperate for the next leg.
teaser: Corn consolidates Monday's China bounce as planting runs ahead of schedule nationwide.

one_number.unit: U.S. corn planted as of Sunday
one_number.context: Six percentage points ahead of the five-year average of 70%, which explains why weather premiums aren't building yet. The Belt is on schedule, no stress, no premium. The funds are positioned for the next weather event, but the calendar says June for heat stress to matter.

yesterdays_call.summary: China deal gives grains structural support the carry trade wasn't pricing.
yesterdays_call.note: Corn held the $4.79 level exactly as the structural support thesis predicted.

sections.title: Grains Hold China Floor
sections.body: Corn closed exactly where it opened at $4.79, the second session of consolidation after Monday's China bounce. The **76% planting pace** removes weather premium from the near-term equation, funds know this. Soybeans gained a penny to $12.17 on steady meal demand, November beans up two cents to $12.05 as the carry structure stays intact. The range is $4.70 to $4.85 in corn until weather matters, which the calendar says is June heat, not May mud.
sections.catalyst: USDA crop progress shows corn planting at 76%, ahead of 70% five-year average
sections.bottom_line: China deal holds the floor, weather calendar controls the ceiling.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: exactly the structural support behavior the China deal was supposed to deliver

sections.title: Cattle Drift Lower on Feed Costs
sections.body: Live cattle eased 75 cents to $247.12, feeder cattle down $2.50 to $358.77, both giving back ground as grain strength weighs on margins. The **feeder weakness** is the tell here, cash feeders still above $245 but the June contract acting heavy. Beef demand stays strong according to wholesale cutouts, but input costs are biting. The China beef facility access from Monday's deal helps long-term, doesn't change today's feed math.
sections.catalyst: China beef facility access restored in Trump-Xi meeting
sections.bottom_line: Strong beef demand meets rising feed costs, feeders show the strain first.

outside_the_pit.title: Deere Right-to-Repair Settlement Gets Court Approval
outside_the_pit.body: Federal court granted preliminary approval to John Deere's antitrust settlement with farmers, giving producers until September to object. The right-to-repair fight isn't going away, but this settles one piece of the legal puzzle.

outside_the_pit.title: Asia's Energy Crunch Opens Door for Australian LNG
outside_the_pit.body: Middle East supply disruptions are pushing Asian buyers toward Australian natural gas as a more reliable alternative. The shift could reshape global energy flows for years, not just months.

outside_the_pit.title: Nigeria Seeks New Oil Buyers as UAE Leaves OPEC
outside_the_pit.body: UAE's OPEC exit is forcing Nigeria to hunt for new crude customers, disrupting the cartel's production balance. Another sign that OPEC discipline is cracking under geopolitical pressure.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: The carry structure is holding despite Monday's rally, which means the funds see this as a short-term China bounce, not a structural shift. If planting stays on pace, this spread widens back to 25 cents by Memorial Day.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming on ethanol demand
basis.body: **Ethanol plants** are coming back online after maintenance season, tightening local corn supplies east of the Mississippi. Western Belt staying soft as river logistics improve. Producers with old-crop storage east of I-35 have a window the futures board isn't fully pricing.

weekly_thread.question: Will China's $17 billion ag commitment shift fund positioning back into grains this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: The commitment is holding corn above $4.70, but funds aren't adding length yet. They're waiting to see if weather cooperates with the demand story.

the_more_you_know.title: Why 76% planted kills weather premium (for now)
the_more_you_know.body: Today's USDA number shows corn planting six points ahead of average, which explains why weather premium isn't building. **The market prices stress, not success.** When planting runs ahead of schedule, the probability of prevented plant acres drops, and so does the weather risk premium. The premium comes back in June when heat matters for pollination, but May wetness doesn't move futures when 76% is already in the ground. The funds know this math.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales: corn under 800K MT keeps China deal skeptics in play
USDA crop progress: corn above 80% planted locks in the no-weather-premium trade
Memorial Day weekend weather models for June heat pattern setup

daily_quote.text: The big money is not in the buying and selling, but in the waiting.

source_summary: USDA crop progress reports, daily futures settlements, China trade commitments
